lines.text: *The Endstart.* The title is chapter 2's title flipped. Chapter 2 was *The Startend.* Chapter 23 is *The Endstart.* Strand opens the chapter by collapsing the distinction. *Startend. Endstart. So what.*

And the epigraph is one of the most metaphysically blunt passages in the book. *The present moment doesn't exist. By the time you notice it, it's already the past. You have never once been really there.* Strand is dismantling the reader's last anchor — the assumption that *now* is a real place.

And then the line that ties this monologue to chapter 22's. *Just energy, moving through meat. Or metal. Or rock.* In chapter 22 Strand said her next vessel would be the tin can. Here she generalizes — *every person you've ever loved was a stranger once.* Identity is a story electricity tells itself.

And the bit that lands hardest. *But here's the thing about dying: Most people are terrified of it. Spend their whole lives building walls against it. And then when it finally comes, they're not really there. They're somewhere else. Looking back. The way they always were.* The chapter is asserting that the dying are *already absent* — and that this is, in a sense, what life has always been.

And then Strand's confession. *But then it happened to me. And now I lie awake and think: Did it? Did it happen to me? Or am I just the story electricity told itself about the caves it used to flow through?* She doesn't know whether she actually died. She just knows the story continues.
*One cave. Another cave. Caves all the way.* The geological cosmology — turtles all the way down, but caves. Hollows. Spaces electricity flows through and forgets.
*So what.* Strand ending on the chapter's two most defiant words. The book's first-person voice has stopped explaining and started shrugging.

Cut to *33 CLICKS POST UNFOLDING. FNS BRISINGER GROUNDFALL.* The *Brisinger* has hit the planet. Oliver is inside the wreck. And the chapter writes the wreck as *terrain* — Hydroponics Bay's toppled racks, broken nutrient lines, rust-colored Mairee soil mixing with ship metal under red emergency lights.

And Læsa is back. Last seen in chapter 11. Now barefoot on broken glass, leaving bloody footprints, *didn't flinch as the shards bit into her heels.* The chapter has reset Læsa's body language — she is *not registering* the kinds of pain a human body should register.

And the *three bodies.* Oliver feels nausea. Læsa just tilts her head. *They're very quiet. They stopped playing.* Læsa is using *child's grammar* to describe corpses. The chapter is reminding us — Læsa is not the species she looks like.

And then the toxic mist. The chapter writes it as a sensory-bleed event. *A sickly mist clung to the floor ahead.* Oliver, rational, posits *coolant or nutrient chemicals reacting with Mairee's spore-filled air.* Læsa, perceptual, says *Bad smoke. It hates the red dust.*

The chapter is offering two ontologies of the same phenomenon. Oliver's is chemistry. Læsa's is animism — the mist has preferences. *It hates the red dust.* And the chapter picks a winner. It just lets Oliver collapse and Læsa save him.

*She gave a short, swift stomp on his chest. In response, Oliver gasped in clean air.* The kind of healing that doesn't come from a Med-Post. Læsa's body is doing something the colony's medicine can't.

And the chapter delivers Læsa's most affectionate line of the book. *You buzz, like a scared little bug.* In chapter 11 she said the world was *crunchy* and the colors *rough.* Now Oliver is a *scared little bug.* The vocabulary hasn't changed.

They climb out to the catwalk. And the chapter delivers the spectacle. *They were hopping with Shunts — an infantry propulsion harness engineered by the same people who had made the Hopper. Whump. One jumped. Whump. The second followed.* Twenty guardians, propulsion-jumping toward the wreck.
And then. *A ripple burst through the air. All at once, they dropped like dead flies.* The chapter delivers a mass kill in two sentences. No explanation. No weapon. Just a *ripple.*

*Each of their bodies hitting the ground kicked up a small cloud of dust, powdering their black figures with rust.* The chapter writes the death as *integration into the landscape.* The guardians are not killed. They are *absorbed.*
And Læsa's commentary. *Our nest is finally quiet. He hates those black wasps. Now, something beautiful will grow there. Brushstrokes of a new beginning.*
*He hates.* That's the second time the chapter has Læsa say *he hates* — first the mist, now the wasps. Both times she means *Æzal.* The entity inside Captain Gupta.

And then the appearance. *A faint glow rose from a breach in the wreckage. An angelic figure appeared. His skin glowed with swirling geometric light. He raised his arms, and from his back, large wings unfolded.*
*The ship started to hum softly. More crew members emerged from other openings in the hull, drifting toward the glowing man like a flock returning to its shepherd.* This is *Æzal*. Wearing wings now.
The shepherd metaphor. The crew is *a flock.*

And Læsa's hand on Oliver's chest. *Don't be scared. Even inside the storm, it's all rainbows.* The chapter is staging a moment of *contact.* Læsa is asking Oliver to *trust* what he is seeing without understanding it.

And then the rescue interrupts the moment. *The whine of maneuvering thrusters high above. A shadow dropped through the turquoise sky like a slice of polished night.* The Stingray. Stacey and Alistair, last seen plunging through Mairee's atmosphere at the end of chapter 20.

And Oliver pulls *a small device* from his rucksack. Activates it. *The shimmering shadow above them reacted, dropping lower.* The COG Egg. Or a beacon synced to it. The Stringray is collecting Oliver, his devices, and Læsa in one extraction.
And the last line of the scene. *Where are we flying to, Dusty?* Læsa, pressed against Oliver in the harness, asking the question of someone who has never been outside a wreck.
Cut to the *CRYO-BAY · FREYA HOMESTEAD.* And the chapter pulls Astrid Vilde out of stasis.

*Supported by Joana, Astrid Vilde took slow, small steps toward the cryo-bay exit. Each one was an act of will.* The chapter writes Vilde's revival as a *body that does not yet remember it is alive.* Joana has dressed her in *a gray-weave shirt from the boy patient* — the same patient whose pod Joana was tending in chapter 21. The chapter is closing that loop.
*Her bare feet dragged against the deck.* Vilde is a Commander reduced to a woman in borrowed boys's clothes. The colony's moral anchor is *carried* out of the bay.
Æzal's second coming coincides with Vilde's. Læsa's bare feet. Vilde too.
Shepards.
The transporter arrives. *A flatbed transporter skidded sideways and slammed to a halt directly in front of them, a red-haired girl at the wheel.* Lysanne.
*Joana! In the back!* And the chapter delivers the gut punch. Ian is in the back. Slumped against tarps. *One hand was clamped to his shoulder, where a dark stain had spread across his jumpsuit.*
*Guardians. Gunshot, through-and-through. I failed to stop him. Olly. Our plan. I thought I could—* Wait. Failed to stop him? Olly?
Did Ian intend to stop Lysanne and Oliver from carrying out their plan? Is that why he was there? When the guardians intercepted Olly and Lysanne?
Or did Ian call in those guardians himself? But why?!
In any case. He evidently had a change of heart. And Vilde, disoriented, asks the question that is on my mind right now. *Who are you?*

Cut to the Med-Post. *The doors peeled apart, and a bright white light spilled out.* Hands take Ian without ceremony. Fatima steps in. *Emergency room.* And then the chapter delivers the relief moment. Joana asks about Su and Zoë. *Asleep. Both of them.* Fatima's *gentle*.
And then the chapter does its closing reveal. Vilde, exhausted, looks through a half-drawn curtain.
She saw a small girl sleeping on a cot. Su. Next to her, a young woman with bruises along her cheek was also asleep. Zoë. The day care nurse Jax assaulted. Possibly raped. And then —

*A guardian sat in the chair between them, wearing armor that was unfastened at the collar. Her helmet rested on the floor, and her short, dark curls looked a little damp. The girl didn't look like a guardian on duty at all.*

*Her shoulders curled forward inside the armor. Her eyes were on the floor between her boots. One hand rested on the young woman's wrist. She did not seem to know she was still holding it.* The chapter is staging Samantha — *Sam* — at Zoë's bedside without naming her. The reader has to *recognize* the description.
Short, dark *curls*. That's the tell. In chapter 18 Jax called her over. *What now, Sam?* A woman with short curls stepped up next to him. Same hair.

Chapter 18 ended Sam's arc with a single sentence. *Samantha did not.* Jax walked away from the nursery, bloody-knuckled, the other recruits trailing him. Samantha did not. She stayed. And the chapter left her there — frozen at the threshold of an action she could not take.

Five chapters later, the chapter answers the question chapter 18 left open. *What did Sam do, after she did nothing?* And here is the answer. She is in the room with Zoë now. Helmet on the floor. Collar unfastened. Holding Zoë's wrist.
Sam went back in. Took her to the med-post and hasn't left her side. Still holding her. We can all feel what Sam is feeling. Guilt. Guilt for not having made the attemt to stop Jax.
And the chapter ends there.
The Endstart.
